mnemonics.title: A-S-D Diagram Selection
mnemonics.content: Remember: Activity = 'Action flow', Sequence = 'Time-based messages', State = 'State transitions'. Use ASD: Activity for workflow, Sequence for timing, State for object lifecycle.

mnemonics.title: SWIM Keys
mnemonics.content: SWIM in activity diagrams: Swimlane = Who does it, Wait/Decision = branches, In/Out = start/end, Merge = combine branches. Swimlanes organize 'who' does 'what'.

mnemonics.title: FORK-JOIN Flow
mnemonics.content: FORK splits one flow into many, JOIN waits for all to complete before continuing. Remember: Fork = 'branch out', Join = 'come together'.

mnemonics.title: STATE Machine Remember
mnemonics.content: States show how objects BEHAVE over TIME. Events trigger changes. Guard conditions control when. Transitions connect states.

acronyms.usage: The standard language for behavioral modeling diagrams including activity, sequence, and state machine diagrams.
Structured analysis technique for behavioral modeling showing how data moves through processes (alternative to UML).

acronyms.expansion: User, Machine, Language - for Sequence diagrams
acronyms.usage: A memory hook: Sequence diagrams show how Users interact with Machines through Language (messages).

visualTips.visualization: Think of it like a flowchart: Start → Actions (rounded rectangles) → Decisions (diamonds) → Parallel paths (forks/joins) → End. Activities flow like water through pipes.
Vertical timeline from top (earlier) to bottom (later). Objects are columns, messages are horizontal arrows. The longer the vertical gap between messages, the longer the wait.
States are like 'modes' of operation. Think of a phone: Silent Mode, Normal Mode, Airplane Mode. Transitions are like 'buttons pressed' that change the mode.
Gatekeepers at doors. The transition can only pass through if the guard condition is satisfied. [balance > 0] is like a gatekeeper checking your balance before letting you withdraw.

analogies.analogy: Structural is like a blueprint of a house (walls, rooms, dimensions). Behavioral is like the instruction manual showing how to use the house (how to open doors, operate AC, flow through rooms).

Think of an activity diagram like a factory assembly line: Products (work items) move through stations (activities), workers in different sections (swimlanes) handle different tasks, decisions act as quality checkpoints.
Traffic lights are perfect state machines: States = Red/Yellow/Green, Events = Timer expires/Button pressed, Transitions = Change color. You can't go from Red directly to Green without Yellow.
Think of a sequence diagram like an email chain: Each person is a column (object), time flows top to bottom, messages are emails sent between people, replies come back as return messages.
When an order comes in (fork), the kitchen prepares different items in parallel (grill, salad, dessert). All items must be ready (join) before the order is served to the customer.
